EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very heavy long period swell moved into the Oregon coastal waters, bringing heavy surf to the coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Buoy 46027 off of Point Saint George recorded swell sufficient to generate heavy surf with breaker heights up to 31 feet along the coast during this interval. Due to the travel time to the coast from the buoy, the heavy surf likely lasted a bit longer than recorded here.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An incoming front brought windy conditions to south central Oregon, and one location reported a gust that met High Wind Warning criteria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Summer Lake RAWS recorded a gust to 60 mph at 23/0938 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Erickson helicopter pilot was killed when his aircraft crashed in a wooded area near Interstate 5 at an elevation of 4100 feet.  The time of the crash is unknown, but it occurred in the morning and the pilot took off from Medford airport at 8:45 PST.    Weather conditions at the time included showers, low ceilings, obscuration of terrain, and significant mountain wave turbulence. The cause of the crash was unknown and will likely remain unknown at least until the investigation is complete.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An Erickson helicopter pilot was killed when his aircraft crashed in a wooded area near Interstate 5 at an elevation of 4100 feet.  The time of the crash is unknown, but it occurred in the morning and the pilot took off from Medford airport at 8:45 PST.    Weather conditions at the time included showers, low ceilings, obscuration of terrain, and significant mountain wave turbulence. The cause of the crash was unknown and will likely remain unknown at least until the investigation is complete.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another in a series of fronts brought high winds to a few locations along the southern Oregon coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ODOT sensor at Humbug Mountain recorded several gusts exceeding 57 mph during this interval.  The peak gust was 65 mph, recorded at 18/2146 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moving up the coast from the south brought strong southerly winds across all of northwest Oregon.  The strongest winds were recorded along the coast, but winds in the Willamette Valley weren't much weaker than what was observed along the coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather station on Mt Hebo recorded wind gusts up to 79 mph. The Tidewater RAWS also recorded wind gusts up to 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A particularly strong atmospheric river took aim for the south Willamette Valley, sitting over areas south of Salem for two days, producing anywhere from 2.5 to 5 inches of rain over a 48 hour period.  Some areas in the Cascades and Cascade Foothills saw 5 to 7 inches of rain over that 48 hour period.  Heavy rain combined with snow melt from all the snow from a few weeks prior in this same area caused flooding along most of our rivers in this area as well as along the main-stem Willamette River up to around Oregon City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Middle Fork of the Willamette River near Jasper crested at 10.5 feet around 4 am on April 12th, which is 0.5 foot above flood stage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather station on Megler Bridge recorded wind gusts up to 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moving up the coast from the south brought strong southerly winds across all of northwest Oregon.  The strongest winds were recorded along the coast, but winds in the Willamette Valley weren't much weaker than what was observed along the coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather station on the Yaquina Bridge recorded sustained winds up to 53 mph with gusts up to 64 mph. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gold Beach ASOS recorded a gust to 59 mph at 06/0359 PST.  A spotter 1SE Brookings reported a gust to 70 mph at 05/1430 PST. A utility company reported a tree down on a power line on Carpenterville Road.  The outage affected 500 customers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought high winds to many locations in southwest and south central Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Buckhorn RAWS recorded a gust to 58 mph at 05/1552 PST. A member of the public in White City reported a tree falling on power lines, causing power outages. Law enforcement reported a tree on the roof of a home on Upper Applegate Road.  A large tree was uprooted on North Main street in Ashland.  A 40-foot tree fell on a fence and shed on Glendower Street in Ashland.  A healthy 15 foot tree 6-8 inches in diameter fell 2NNE Medford. Power outages affected approximately 1500 customers in Jackson County.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES OF THE OREGON CASCADES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong winds aloft were transported to the surface over portions of central Oregon during the morning of January 23rd via both mountain wave and downslope mechanisms. Significant tree damage and power outages were reported between La Pine and Sunriver and in the Sisters area. While in-situ wind measurements are sparse in these areas, the Sisters airport reported a gust of 64 knots (74 mph) and a weather spotter near Three Rivers measured a gust of 61 knots (70 mph) with a handheld anemometer. Isolated marginally severe gusts around 50 knots (58 mph) were also observed along some of the more exposed ridge tops of north central Oregon. The upper slopes of Mt Bachelor also saw winds approaching 87 knots (100 mph) that prompted closure of all ski lifts.
Mostly clear skies and a cold air mass allowed temperatures to drop below freezing along the Oregon coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported low temperatures in this zone ranged from 28 to 35 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An incoming front brought high winds to the Oregon coast and Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ODOT sensor at Humbug Mountain recorded several gusts exceeding 57 mph during this interval.  The peak gust was 68 mph recorded at 17/0601 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weekend of very heavy rain led to river rises across southern Oregon. The Rogue River at Agness exceeded flood stage on this day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rogue River at Agness exceeded flood stage (17.0 feet) at 20/0545 PST. The river crested at 19.36 feet at 20/1215 PST. The river dropped below flood stage at 20/2145 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weekend of heavy rain led to flooding on the Coquille River at Coquille.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Coquille River at Coquille exceeded minor flood stage (21.0 feet) at 20/1200 PST.  It exceeded moderate flood stage (23.0) feet at 21/1300 PST and dropped below moderate flood stage at 21/1430 PST.  The river dropped below minor flood stagat at 22/1515 PST.

Some areas in the Cascades and Cascade Foothills saw 5 to 7 inches of rain over that 48 hour period. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A level 3 evacuation was ordered for the 100-year flood plain along the Row River near Cottage Grove.  The release of water from the Dorena Reservoir was higher than recorded in the 1996 flood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An incoming front brought high winds to parts of the coast and east of the Cascades.

Interstate 84 between Pendleton and La Grande was closed for several hours due to heavy snow and near zero visibility.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain (by April standards) combined with snowmelt caused a number of area rivers to flood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The river rose above minor flood stage (17.0 feet) at 08/0900 PST. The river crested at 21.93 feet at 08/2145 PST.  The river fell below minor flood stage at 09/2000 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An incoming front brought high winds to parts of the coast and east of the Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Summer Lake RAWS reported numerous gusts exceeding 57 mph between 06/2339 PST and 07/1038 PST. The peak gust was 66 mph reported at 07/0638 PST. The Rock Creek RAWS reported a gust to 59 mph at 07/1227 PST and a gust to 58 mph at 07/1627 PST.  A spotter at Silver Lake reported sustained winds estimated at 40 mph with whole trees in motion. Walking was also impeded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain along with the melting of recent snowfall caused flooding at several locations in southern Oregon in late February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rogue River at Agness rose above flood stage (17.0 feet) at 25/2100 PST, crested at 18.32 feet at 26/0615 PST, then fell below flood stage at 26/1215 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain along with the melting of recent snowfall caused flooding at several locations in southern Oregon in late February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The North Fork of the Coquille River at Myrtle Point rose above flood stage (35.0 feet) at 25/0700 PST.  It exceeded moderate flood stage (36.0 feet) at 25/1515 PST. then crested at 36.02 feet at 25/1600 PST. It fell below moderate flood stage at 25/1730 PST, then fell below flood stage at 26/0100 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain along with the melting of recent snowfall caused flooding at several locations in southern Oregon in late February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The South Fork of the Coquille River at Myrtle Point rose above flood stage (33.0 feet) at 25/0050 PST, exceeded moderate flood stage (35.0 feet) at 25/0400 PST, then exceeded major flood stage (37.0 feet) at 25/0810 PST.  The river crested at 37.81 feet at 25/1330 PST.  The river fell below major flood stage at 25/1950 PST, fell below moderate flood stage at 26/0310 PST, then fell below flood stage at 26/1020 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain along with the melting of recent snowfall caused flooding at several locations in southern Oregon in late February.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Coquille River at Coquille rose above flood stage (21.0 feet) at 25/0430 PST, exceeded moderate flood stage (23.0 feet) at 25/1045 PST, then crested at 24.38 feet at 26/0230 PST.  The river fell below moderate flood stage at 27/1500 PST, then fell below flood stage at 28/1745 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several storms in February brought heavy snow to unusually low elevations, making a great impact in some areas.   This was the first of the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter 6E Gold Beach at elevation 2627 feet reported snow beginning overnight on the morning of the 8th.  An estimated 2.0 inches was on the ground the morning of the 9th.  The spotter then reported a measured 10.3 inches on the ground at 10/0800 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moving up the coast from the south brought strong southerly winds across all of northwest Oregon.  The strongest winds were recorded along the coast, but winds in the Willamette Valley weren't much weaker than what was observed along the coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Portland Airport ASOS recorded wind gusts up to 54 mph.  Chehalem Mtn and a couple other high spots with high exposure did locally reach 60+ mph, but most saw wind gusts mainly up to 54 mph. At one point there were over 100,000 people without power.  PGE reported that 150 power lines were brought down by strong wind.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moving up the coast from the south brought strong southerly winds across all of northwest Oregon.  The strongest winds were recorded along the coast, but winds in the Willamette Valley weren't much weaker than what was observed along the coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Salem Airport ASOS recorded wind gusts up to 54 mph.  6500 people lost power during the peak of this storm, as several power lines went down in south Salem, Stayton, Dallas, and Independence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moderately strong atmospheric river brought heavy rain to the area Friday, January 19.  Heavy rain fell during the afternoon through Friday night.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused a three-car accident at mile post 3 on Highway 211/Molalla Road in Clackamas County. One person died in the crash.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm brought a mix of wintry precipitation to the region on the 14th of February. Over central Oregon and eastern Oregon the precipitation type was primarily freezing rain and sleet with generally light accumulations. Further north and west over north central Oregon and the Columbia River Gorge heavy snow fell with storm total accumulations of 4 to 7 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The cooperative observer at Toketee Falls reported 7.0 inches of snow in 24 hours ending at 09/2000 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another front moved through, and heavy precipitation persisted over some of the west side valleys of southwest Oregon as snow levels lowered. This led to a closure of Interstate 5 due to heavy snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow showers led to the closure of Interstate 5 from Hugo north to Canyonville from 15/1930 through 15/2300 PST.  This stretch included four passes that topped out between 1500 and 2000 feet above sea level.  The passes got around 3 inches of snow over a short period of time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pair of storm systems brought significant snow to all elevations on the 3rd and 4th of February. Wraparound moisture from the first system brought 8 to 12 inches of snow to the Blue Mountains as well as the Grande Ronde and Wallowa Valleys. Initial precipitation with the second system combined with lingering wraparound moisture brought between 4 and 7 inches to all elevations on the 4th of February. Numerous accidents were reported due to slippery conditions. Interstate 84 for closed for several hours between Baker City and Pendleton during the day the the 4th to clear multiple accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow water equivalents near 200% of normal in the Blue Mountains coupled with warm temperatures and near record rainfall totals for April produced significant river flooding across eastern Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Silvies River overflowed its banks with widespread field and road flooding in and around Seneca. Canyon Creek flooded into fields above Highway 395. Through the town on Canyon City, the high water damaged one bridge over Canyon Creek and 2 homes. The high water on the John Day River caused several state highways to be closed between Dayville, Picture Gorge and Kimberly. Clyde Holliday Park on the outskirts of John Day was flooded. Other creeks and streams caused numerous road washouts across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow water equivalents near 200% of normal in the Blue Mountains coupled with warm temperatures and near record rainfall totals for April produced significant river flooding across eastern Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous sections of Oregon Highway 19 and 207 were closed due to water over road and damage to road surfaces. Numerous county roads were damaged with rock and mudslides as well as plugged culverts. Flooding was reported in the city park in Spray.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow water equivalents near 200% of normal in the Blue Mountains coupled with warm temperatures and near record rainfall totals for April produced significant river flooding across eastern Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding along Hinton Creek took place on April 9th and 10th. The fairgrounds in Heppner were flooded as well as fields along the creek above Heppner.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow water equivalents near 200% of normal in the Blue Mountains coupled with warm temperatures and near record rainfall totals for April produced significant river flooding across eastern Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A levy along Camas Creek in Ukiah was damaged with flooding of several outbuildings on properties in Ukiah, several homes were surrounded with water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow water equivalents near 200% of normal in the Blue Mountains coupled with warm temperatures and near record rainfall totals for April produced significant river flooding across eastern Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was widespread flooding of fields and lowlands along the river across Union County.  There were at least 5000 acres of agricultural land flooded and numerous roads had damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow water equivalents near 200% of normal in the Blue Mountains coupled with warm temperatures and near record rainfall totals for April produced significant river flooding across eastern Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Field and roadway flooding was seen along the Grande Ronde River and numerous other small streams with significant snowmelt and heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow water equivalents near 200% of normal in the Blue Mountains coupled with warm temperatures and near record rainfall totals for April produced significant river flooding across eastern Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: McKay Creek, with headwaters in the Blue Mountains saw significant rises with record inflows recorded at McKay Reservoir in Pendleton. This resulted in increased releases from the McKay Dam, with flooding downstream along the creek through Pendleton. Teams were out in partnership with Team Rubicon and surveyed 102 homes in the McKay area. Nine homes reported flooded basements, twenty-eight homes reported subfloor or foundation flooding and sixty-five homes had water encroachment on the property. Over ninety homes had actively sandbagged their property.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow water equivalents near 200% of normal in the Blue Mountains coupled with warm temperatures and near record rainfall totals for April produced significant river flooding across eastern Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of high flows from rain and snow melt coming out of the Blue Mountains and record releases out of McKay Reservoir produced field and roadside flooding downstream of Pendleton through Echo, Hermiston and Umatilla. A footbridge at Umatilla was damaged by the high flows and erosion.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent troughing off the coast of the Pacific Northwest focused a stream of mid-level moisture over the Inland Northwest resulting in a long duration snow event as the plume drifted north and south several times between the 22nd and 27th of February. Snowfall rates were greatly enhanced over central Oregon with the proximity of a nearly stationary surface boundary where snowfall rates were in excess of 1 inch per hour. Storm total snowfall amounts were measured at: 40 inches in Sisters, 33 inches in Bend, 30 inches in Redmond, 26 inches in Meacham, 22 inches in Prineville, 21 inches in Elgin, 16 inches in Mitchell, 14 inches in Lostine and La Grande, 12 inches in Pendleton and Joseph and 10 inches in John Day. In Bend a few roofs collapsed under the weight of the snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent troughing off the coast of the Pacific Northwest focused a stream of mid-level moisture over the Inland Northwest resulting in a long duration snow event as the plume drifted north and south several times between the 22nd and 27th of February. Snowfall rates were greatly enhanced over central Oregon with the proximity of a nearly stationary surface boundary where snowfall rates were in excess of 1 inch per hour. Storm total snowfall amounts were measured at: 40 inches in Sisters, 33 inches in Bend, 30 inches in Redmond, 26 inches in Meacham, 22 inches in Prineville, 21 inches in Elgin, 16 inches in Mitchell, 14 inches in Lostine and La Grande, 12 inches in Pendleton and Joseph and 10 inches in John Day. In Bend a few roofs collapsed under the weight of the snow.

An unusually cold Arctic front settled into the region mid to late October, setting the stage for an early winter weather scenario. As a secondary storm system and cold front interacted with the mountains of northeast Oregon, moderate to heavy snow developed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A foot of snow fell at the Milk Shakes SNOTEL. Elevation is 5600 feet in the Blue Mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of marginal instability and strong shear brought a few strong thunderstorms to Gilliam and Wasco County on the afternoon of June 26th. In Condon hail accumulations of up to 2 inches was reported while the largest hailstone topped out around nickel size. Further southeast in Wasco county wind gusts of 55 to 57 mph were reported. This event was covered by a severe thunderstorm watch, the last watch that was issued for the Inland Northwest occurred 780 days prior.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to nickel size and .62 inches of rainfall in 15 minutes was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent trough of low pressure situated over northern Nevada coupled with warm, moist air aided in severe thunderstorm development, including a tornado, over parts of Southeast Oregon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Back-to-back low pressure systems dropping south along the coast of British Columbia and Washington brought cold air south into NW Oregon as well as plenty of moisture.  Locations in the Columbia Gorge and Hood River Valley saw 1 to 2 feet of snow.  At lower elevations, a near stationary frontal boundary the night of the 11th into the morning of the 12th dropped several inches of rain over portions of Washington, Columbia, Tillamook and Yamhill Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: While little-to-no snow fell at lower elevations, Seine Creek SNOTEL around 2000 feet recorded 8 inches of snow in a 7-hour period.

Heavy snow and ice closed I-84 in both directions east of Milepost 23 due to heavy snow and ice which caused a crash involving multiple tractor-trailers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system dropped south along the coast from Victoria Island.  The low pressure system brought arctic air south out of Canada into the Columbia Basin, which was then funneled into the northern Willamette Valley.  This system brought heavy snow to the Coast Range and the Cascade Foothills, the Columbia Gorge, and Hood River Valley.  It also brought snow down to the Valley floor near the Columbia Gorge in the Portland Metro area.  Other areas on the south and west side of the Portland Metro area did see some periods of lighter snow but not much if any snow accumulated on the ground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most precipitation fell as rain or a rain/snow mix at river level, but elevations above 500 feet observed more snow. CoCoRaHS observers reported around 5 inches of snow near Alston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved into far northern California, this time really only bringing heavy snow to the south Willamette Valley.  Cold air was already in place due to snow still on the ground from the previous snow event earlier in the week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were several public reports of 4 to 8 inches of snow.  This storm hampered recovery efforts from the February 24-25 snow storm, extending power outages for many customers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moist southerly flow, surface heating, and an upper air disturbance combined to create an environment favorable for thunderstorm development. Many did develop, and at least one reached severe status.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported their house was shaken by strong winds that came first from one direction, then from the opposite direction.  They reported 1 inch hail with the second wind surge.  The winds sounded like several freight trains.  No damage to the house, but there was damage to vegetation with some large tree branches found far away from any trees.  This occurred on the Lost River Ranch.  The observer thought it may have been a tornado.  A funnel cloud associated with another thunderstorm was reported at 1417 PDT the previous afternoon near Klamath Falls.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHS observer 1 mile SW of Hood River recorded 15.7 inches of snow in 24 hours. Another CoCoRaHS observer in Hood River recorded 11 inches of snow in 24 hours. Per the Oregonian (newspaper), Interstate 84 was closed in both directions east of Milepost 23 at 528 AM on February 12th due to snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure aloft forced a thermal trough near the coast to move inland, bringing hot and dry conditions to the inland west side valleys in southwest Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The only reported high temperature in this zone on 08/27 was 105 degrees at the Illinois Valley RAWS.  Reported highs ranged from 84 to 98 degrees on 08/28.  Low temperatures on the morning of 08/28 ranged from 57 to 72 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moving up from the southwest brought moisture up the east side of the Cascades, wrapping around into the Columbia Gorge.  This combined with cold air pulled into the Columbia Gorge from the Columbia Basin brought heavy snow to the Columbia Gorge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Cooperative observer reported 5.8 inches over a 24 hour period near Cascade Locks.  Locations along the Washington side had CoCoRaHS reports of 3 to 5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad low pressure system that had been sitting off the coast of Washington and Oregon moved onto the coast, bringing with it plenty of moisture from the Pacific Ocean.  With a cold air mass in place, heavy snow fell in the Lane County Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Salt Creek Falls SNOTEL recorded 26 inches of snow, and Cascade Summit SNOTEL recorded 25 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Man drowns at Nehalem Bay State Park after being swept out by rip current.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tillamook County Sheriff's officer reported that a swimmer died at Nehalem Bay State Park after being swept out to sea by a rip current.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system dropped south along the coast from Victoria Island.  The low pressure system brought arctic air south out of Canada into the Columbia Basin, which was then funneled into the northern Willamette Valley.  This system brought heavy snow to the Coast Range and the Cascade Foothills, the Columbia Gorge, and Hood River Valley.  It also brought snow down to the Valley floor near the Columbia Gorge in the Portland Metro area.  Other areas on the south and west side of the Portland Metro area did see some periods of lighter snow but not much if any snow accumulated on the ground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulations ranged from 6 to 12 inches. Amounts were higher closer to Bonneville, where the COOP observer reported 12 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: It started as a low pressure system moving south into eastern Washington, bringing a cold front southeastward across western Oregon.  The front then stalled across the southern Willamette Valley and Lane County Cascades as colder and colder air moved in aloft. What started as rain at low elevations turned to snow during the afternoon of the 23rd. The stalled front kept producing snow over the same areas through the next 24 hours with a direct tap of moisture from the Pacific Ocean. Another surface low pressure system rode up the stationary front and brought another surge of moisture and heavy snow on the 24th through the morning of the 25th. This surface low finally pushed the frontal boundary out of the area and brought an end to the snow the night of the 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow totals were generally 8 to 16 inches, with locations as low as the valley floors falling within this range.  CoCoRaHS observers from Lorane and Oakridge each reported 18 inches.  The heavy snow closed Interstate 5 near Cottage Grove and Highway 58 east of Lowell due to multiple accidents and trees downed by the weight of the snow.  Highway 58 was shut down for several days.  Power outages lasted several days in some locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: It started as a low pressure system moving south into eastern Washington, bringing a cold front southeastward across western Oregon.  The front then stalled across the southern Willamette Valley and Lane County Cascades as colder and colder air moved in aloft. What started as rain at low elevations turned to snow during the afternoon of the 23rd. The stalled front kept producing snow over the same areas through the next 24 hours with a direct tap of moisture from the Pacific Ocean. Another surface low pressure system rode up the stationary front and brought another surge of moisture and heavy snow on the 24th through the morning of the 25th. This surface low finally pushed the frontal boundary out of the area and brought an end to the snow the night of the 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heaviest snow was down around Santiam Pass, which had 12 inches of snow in 12 hours.  Many other locations in the area had 8 to 16 inches of snow. This combined with the heavy snow on the 24th resulted in the closure of Highway 20 at Milepost 38.5.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm caused by a rapidly deepening bomb cyclone brought heavy snow and high winds to the area, creating blizzard-like conditions at times. The cyclone moved onshore near the Oregon/California border with central pressure near 971 mb and then moved east-northeast through the area. Numerous accidents and road closures, including Interstate 5, were reported. Around 8000 customers lost power due to downed power lines. The surface pressure recorded in Medford was the second lowest ever recorded at that station.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The coop observer at Prospect reported 8.0 inches of snow in 24 hours ending at 27/2000 PST.  A spotter 5.4NNW Gold Hill  8.6 inches of snow in 24 hours ending at 27/0700 PST.

Cape Blanco recorded  gusts exceeding 57 mph continuously from 26/0830 PST until 26/1700 PST.  The peak gust was 106 mph, recorded at 26/1330 PST and 26/1345 PST. Downed trees and/or power lines were reported on Oregon state highways 38, 42, 42S, and US Highway 101.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major wind storm caused by a rapidly deepening bomb cyclone brought strong winds to the southern Oregon coast.  Inland, there was also heavy snow, creating blizzard-like conditions at times. The cyclone moved onshore near the Oregon/California border with central pressure near 971 mb and then moved east-northeast through the area. Numerous accidents and road closures, including Interstate 5, were reported. Around 8000 customers lost power due to downed power lines. The surface pressure recorded in Medford was the second lowest ever recorded at that station.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Quail Prairie RAWS recorded gusts exceeding 57 mph continuously during this interval.  The peak gust was 74 mph, recorded at 26/1439 PST and at 26/1539 PST.  The Onion Mountain RAWS recorded a gust to 58 mph at 26/1545 PST.

A spotter 2SSE Pistol River reported estimated gusts to 60 mph between 26/1500 PST and 26/1700 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm moved into the area, and produced an EF-0 Tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS storm survey confirmed an EF-0 tornado with winds of 65 mph, touched down in farmland. This caused damage to the farmland which grew corn and pumpkins. It also damaged barn doors, 19 solar panels, and caused tree damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was reported to the office via broadcast media.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local media partner reported a funnel cloud near Harrisburg, OR.
